content:
# Luke Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 101-102 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_011_page_001.jpg, LUK_011_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And it came to pass, [while] he was in a certain place praying, as he ceased, a certain one of his disciples said to him, "Lord, teach us to pray, just as also John taught his disciples."

**2.** And he said to them, "When you pray, say: 'Father, let your name be hallowed; let your kingdom come;

**3.** our daily bread give to us each day;

**4.** and forgive us our sins, for also we ourselves forgive everyone being indebted to us; and do not bring us into testing.'"

**5.** And he said to them, "Who of you shall have a friend, and shall go to him at midnight, and say to him, 'Friend, lend me three loaves,

**6.** since a friend of mine has come to me from [a] journey, and I do not have what I shall set before him';

**7.** and that [one] from within, having answered, should say, 'Do not cause me trouble; the door has already been shut, and my children are with me in the bed; I am not able, having risen, to give to you'?

**8.** I say to you, even if he will not give to him, having risen, because of being his friend, yet because of his shamelessness, having risen, he will give to him as many as he needs.

**9.** And I say to you, ask, and it shall be given to you; seek, and you shall find; knock, and it shall be opened to you.

**10.** For everyone asking receives, and the [one] seeking finds, and to the [one] knocking it shall be opened.

**11.** And which father among you — [if] the son shall ask for a fish, will he instead of a fish give him a serpent?

**12.** Or also [if] he shall ask for an egg, will he give him a scorpion?

**13.** If therefore you, being evil, know [how] to give good gifts to your children, how much more shall the Father, the [one] out of heaven, give [the] Holy Spirit to those asking him?"

**14.** And he was casting out a demon, and it was mute. And it came to pass, the demon having gone out, the mute [man] spoke; and the crowds marveled.

**15.** But some of them said, "By Beelzebul, the ruler of the demons, he casts out the demons."

**16.** And others, testing [him], were seeking from him a sign out of heaven.

**17.** But he, knowing their thoughts, said to them, "Every kingdom having been divided against itself is made desolate, and house against house falls.

**18.** And if also Satan was divided against himself, how shall his kingdom stand? Because you say [that] by Beelzebul I cast out the demons.

**19.** But if I by Beelzebul cast out the demons, by whom do your sons cast [them] out? On account of this, they shall be your judges.

**20.** But if by [the] finger of God I cast out the demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.

**21.** When the strong [man], having been armed, guards his own courtyard, his possessions are in peace;

**22.** but when [one] stronger than he, having come upon [him], should overcome him, he takes away his full armor upon which he had trusted, and distributes his spoils.

**23.** The [one] not being with me is against me, and the [one] not gathering with me scatters.

**24.** When the unclean spirit goes out from the man, it passes through waterless places, seeking rest; and not finding [it], it says, 'I will return into my house from which I came out.'

**25.** And having come, it finds [the house] having been swept and having been put in order.

**26.** Then it goes and takes along seven other spirits more wicked than itself, and having entered, they dwell there; and the last [state] of that man becomes worse than the first."

**27.** And it came to pass, [while] he [was] saying these [things], a certain woman, having lifted up [her] voice from the crowd, said to him, "Blessed [is] the womb having carried you, and [the] breasts which you sucked."

**28.** But he said, "Rather, blessed [are] those hearing the word of God and keeping [it]."

**29.** And the crowds being gathered together, he began to say, "This generation is an evil generation; it seeks a sign, and a sign shall not be given to it except the sign of Jonah.

**30.** For just as Jonah became a sign to the Ninevites, so also shall the Son of Man be to this generation.

**31.** [The] queen of [the] south shall be raised in the judgment with the men of this generation and shall condemn them; for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, [something] greater than Solomon [is] here.

**32.** [The] men of Nineveh shall stand up in the judgment with this generation and shall condemn it; for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and behold, [something] greater than Jonah [is] here.

**33.** No one, having lit a lamp, puts [it] into a hidden [place] nor under the measuring-basket, but upon the lamp-stand, so that those entering in may see the light.

**34.** The lamp of the body is your eye. When your eye is single, your whole body also is illuminated; but when it is evil, your body also [is] dark.

**35.** Watch therefore, [lest] the light in you is darkness.

**36.** If therefore your whole body [is] illuminated, not having any part dark, it shall be wholly illuminated, as when the lamp by [its] flashing illuminates you."

**37.** And as he spoke, a Pharisee asked him that he might dine with him; and having entered, he reclined.

**38.** And the Pharisee, having seen, marveled that he was not first baptized before the dinner.

**39.** And the Lord said to him, "Now you, the Pharisees, cleanse the outside of the cup and of the dish, but your inside is full of plunder and wickedness.

**40.** Senseless [ones]! Did not the [one] having made the outside also make the inside?

**41.** Nevertheless, give the [things] being within [as] alms, and behold, all [things] are clean to you.

**42.** But woe to you, the Pharisees, because you tithe the mint and the rue and every herb, and you pass by the justice and the love of God; but these [things] it was necessary to do, and those not to pass by.

**43.** Woe to you, the Pharisees, because you love the first-seat in the synagogues and the greetings in the marketplaces.

**44.** Woe to you, because you are as the unseen tombs, and the men walking above [them] do not know [it]."

**45.** And having answered, a certain one of the law-experts says to him, "Teacher, [by] saying these [things], you insult us also."

**46.** And he said, "And to you, the law-experts, woe, because you load the men [with] burdens hard-to-bear, and you yourselves do not touch the burdens with one of your fingers.

**47.** Woe to you, because you build the tombs of the prophets, and your fathers killed them.

**48.** Therefore you are witnesses and you consent to the works of your fathers; because they indeed killed them, and you build [their tombs].

**49.** On account of this also the Wisdom of God said, 'I will send to them prophets and apostles, and [some] of them they shall kill and persecute,'

**50.** so that the blood of all the prophets having been poured out from [the] foundation of [the] world may be required from this generation,

**51.** from [the] blood of Abel unto [the] blood of Zechariah, the [one] having perished between the altar and the house. Yes, I say to you, it shall be required from this generation.

**52.** Woe to you, the law-experts, because you took away the key of the knowledge; you yourselves did not enter, and those entering you hindered."

**53.** And [when] he went out from there, the scribes and the Pharisees began to be terribly hostile and to provoke him to speak about many [things],

**54.** lying in ambush for him, to catch something from his mouth.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **11:2 — The Shorter Lord's Prayer:** Sinaiticus preserves the shorter Lukan form of the Lord's Prayer, lacking several petitions found in Matthew 6:9-13. Absent from Sinaiticus here are: "Our [Father], the [one] in the heavens" (Sinaiticus reads simply Πατερ, "Father"), "let your will be done, as in heaven also upon the earth," and the final "but deliver us from the evil [one]." Later manuscripts harmonized Luke's version toward Matthew's longer form. The shorter reading is supported by Vaticanus (B) and is almost certainly original to Luke.

2. **11:2 — "Father" (Πατερ):** Sinaiticus reads the bare vocative Πατερ without "our" (ημων) or "in the heavens" (ο εν τοις ουρανοις). This unadorned address corresponds to the Aramaic Abba, the intimate familial term that Jesus characteristically used (cf. Mark 14:36; Galatians 4:6; Romans 8:15). The simplicity is striking and likely original.

3. **11:3 — "daily" (επιουσιον):** Sinaiticus reads τον αρτον ημων τον επιουσιον. The word επιουσιος is one of the most debated terms in the New Testament — it appears nowhere in Greek literature outside the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:11 and here). Origin himself noted it seemed coined by the Evangelists. Proposed derivations: (a) from επι + ουσια ("for existence/substance" = necessary for survival), (b) from επι + ιεναι ("for the coming [day]" = tomorrow's bread), (c) from επι + ειναι ("supersubstantial" = the bread of God's presence). Jerome translated it as "supersubstantialem" in Matthew but "quotidianum" ("daily") in Luke. The ambiguity may be intentional.

4. **11:4 — "forgive us our sins" (αφες ημιν τας αμαρτιας ημων):** Sinaiticus reads αμαρτιας ("sins") where Matthew 6:12 reads οφειληματα ("debts"). Luke's second clause retains the debt metaphor — "everyone being indebted (οφειλοντι) to us" — preserving the Aramaic original's wordplay on hoba, which means both "sin" and "debt." Luke thus interprets for his Greek audience while keeping the underlying metaphor.

5. **11:4 — "do not bring us into testing" (μη εισενεγκης ημας εις πειρασμον):** Sinaiticus reads πειρασμον, which can mean "testing," "trial," or "temptation." The aorist subjunctive εισενεγκης with μη functions as a prohibition. The theological difficulty — does God lead people into temptation? — has generated extensive discussion. The verb εισφερω ("to bring/carry into") is causative: the prayer asks not to be brought into a situation of testing, not merely to be preserved within it.

6. **11:8 — "shamelessness" (αναιδειαν):** Sinaiticus reads αναιδειαν, from αναιδεια ("shamelessness/impudence/persistence"). This is a hapax legomenon in the New Testament. The word is debated: does it refer to the shamelessness of the asker (his bold persistence) or the householder's desire to avoid the shame of refusing? In a Mediterranean honor-shame culture, both readings are viable. The parable's point concerns bold confidence in approaching God.

7. **11:11 — Sinaiticus omits the bread/stone saying:** Sinaiticus lacks the clause found in many manuscripts: "shall ask for bread — he will not give him a stone, will he?" (cf. Matthew 7:9). This shorter reading, also found in Papyrus 75 and Vaticanus, moves directly from fish/serpent to egg/scorpion. The bread/stone saying may have been imported from the Matthean parallel by later scribes.

8. **11:13 — "the Father, the [one] out of heaven" (ο πατηρ ο εξ ουρανου):** Sinaiticus reads ο πατηρ ο εξ ουρανου, an unusual construction. Where Matthew 7:11 reads "your Father, the [one] in the heavens," Sinaiticus's Luke reads "the Father, the [one] from heaven" — emphasizing the heavenly origin of the gift rather than the location of the Father. This also aligns with Luke's gift being "Holy Spirit" rather than Matthew's "good things."

9. **11:13 — "Holy Spirit" (πνευμα αγιον):** Sinaiticus reads πνευμα αγιον where the Matthean parallel (7:11) reads δοματα αγαθα ("good gifts"). This is a distinctively Lukan emphasis: the Holy Spirit is the supreme gift the Father gives (cf. Acts 1:4-5, 2:1-4, 2:38). Luke's entire two-volume work traces the Spirit's activity from Jesus' conception to the church's mission.

10. **11:15 — "Beelzebul" (Βεελζεβουλ):** Sinaiticus reads Βεελζεβουλ. The name's etymology is disputed: (a) Baal-zebub ("lord of flies," 2 Kings 1:2), (b) Baal-zebul ("lord of the dwelling/temple"), (c) Baal-zibbul ("lord of dung"). The second option is most likely, as it creates a wordplay with "house" (οικια/οικος) in the subsequent parable. The Pharisees charge that Jesus' exorcisms operate by demonic power — the ruler of the demons' own authority.

11. **11:17 — "knowing their thoughts" (ειδως αυτων τα διανοηματα):** Sinaiticus reads διανοηματα ("thoughts/deliberations"), a rare word occurring only here and in variant readings in the New Testament. The related noun διανοια ("mind/understanding") is more common. Jesus' knowledge of unspoken thoughts is a recurring Lukan motif (5:22, 6:8, 9:47), implying divine insight.

12. **11:20 — "finger of God" (δακτυλω θεου):** Sinaiticus reads δακτυλω θεου where the Matthean parallel (12:28) reads πνευματι θεου ("Spirit of God"). The "finger of God" alludes directly to Exodus 8:19 (LXX 8:15), where Pharaoh's magicians, unable to replicate the plague of gnats, declare "this is the finger of God." The exodus typology is deliberate: as God's finger defeated Egyptian sorcery, so now it defeats demonic power. Many scholars consider "finger of God" the more original reading, later changed to "Spirit of God" in Matthew's theological framework.

13. **11:21-22 — The Strong Man parable:** Sinaiticus reads ο ισχυρος καθωπλισμενος ("the strong [man] having been fully armed"). The verb καθοπλιζω means "to arm completely" — this is not a casual defender but a fully equipped warrior. The "stronger one" (ισχυροτερος) who overcomes him takes his πανοπλιαν ("full armor," a term used in Ephesians 6:11, 13 for the "whole armor of God"). The image is of a complete military defeat and plundering of the vanquished.

14. **11:24 — "waterless places" (ανυδρων τοπων):** Sinaiticus reads δι ανυδρων τοπων ("through waterless places"). In Jewish demonology, demons were believed to inhabit deserts and desolate places (cf. Isaiah 13:21, 34:14; Baruch 4:35; Tobit 8:3). The Dead Sea region's arid wasteland was particularly associated with demonic habitation. The demon's restless wandering through such places before returning underscores the danger of a spiritual vacuum.

15. **11:27 — "having lifted up [her] voice" (επαρασα φωνην):** Sinaiticus reads επαρασα φωνην, literally "having lifted up a voice." This Semitic idiom (Hebrew: nasa qol) means to cry out loudly. The woman pronounces a beatitude on Jesus' mother — "blessed is the womb" — using bodily language typical of Semitic blessing formulas. Jesus redirects the blessing from biological kinship to spiritual obedience.

16. **11:29 — Sinaiticus and the sign of Jonah:** Sinaiticus reads σημειον ου δοθησεται αυτη ει μη το σημειον Ιωνα ("a sign shall not be given to it except the sign of Jonah"). Unlike Matthew 12:40, which explicitly interprets the sign as Jonah's three days in the whale (prefiguring the burial and resurrection), Luke leaves the sign unexplained beyond verse 30: "as Jonah became a sign to the Ninevites." The sign may be Jonah's preaching itself — the prophet who came from apparent death to call a pagan city to repentance.

17. **11:31 — "queen of the south" (βασιλισσα νοτου):** Sinaiticus reads βασιλισσα νοτου. This refers to the Queen of Sheba (1 Kings 10:1-13), who traveled from southern Arabia (or possibly Ethiopia) to hear Solomon's wisdom. The verb εγερθησεται ("shall be raised") in the judgment context carries resurrection overtones — she will literally stand up in the eschatological judgment as a witness against this generation.

18. **11:31-32 — Reversed order from Matthew:** In Sinaiticus (and Luke generally), the Queen of the South appears before the men of Nineveh (11:31-32), whereas Matthew 12:41-42 reverses this order (Nineveh first, then the Queen). Luke's order creates a chiastic structure with the preceding reference to Jonah: Jonah (v.30) — Queen/Solomon (v.31) — Nineveh/Jonah (v.32).

19. **11:33 — "hidden place" (κρυπτην):** Sinaiticus reads εις κρυπτην, "into a hidden [place]" or "into a cellar/crypt." The word κρυπτη can refer to an underground room or vault. This is a Lukan addition to the lamp saying compared with the Matthean parallel. Combined with "nor under the measuring-basket" (μηδε υπο τον μοδιον), two different modes of concealment are rejected.

20. **11:34 — "single" (απλους):** Sinaiticus reads απλους, which literally means "single/simple/without folds." The opposite, πονηρος ("evil/wicked"), is given as the alternative. The "single eye" likely denotes generosity and undivided devotion (cf. the connection between απλοτης and generosity in Romans 12:8, 2 Corinthians 8:2, 9:11), while the "evil eye" in Semitic idiom denotes stinginess and jealousy.

21. **11:37 — "he was not first baptized" (ου πρωτον εβαπτισθη):** Sinaiticus reads εβαπτισθη, the aorist passive of βαπτιζω ("to immerse/wash"). This does not refer to the sacrament of baptism but to the Pharisaic practice of ritual hand-washing (cf. Mark 7:3-4) before meals. Mark 7:4 uses the same verb βαπτιζω for the washing of cups, pitchers, and bronze vessels. The Pharisee's astonishment reveals the cultural expectation of ritual purity observance.

22. **11:39 — "plunder and wickedness" (αρπαγης και πονηριας):** Sinaiticus reads αρπαγης και πονηριας. The Matthean parallel (23:25) reads αρπαγης και ακρασιας ("plunder and self-indulgence"). Luke's πονηρια ("wickedness") is a broader moral category than Matthew's ακρασια ("lack of self-control"). The metaphor of a cup clean outside but filthy inside makes the Pharisaic focus on external ritual purity absurd when internal moral corruption persists.

23. **11:41 — "give the things being within as alms" (τα ενοντα δοτε ελεημοσυνην):** Sinaiticus reads τα ενοντα δοτε ελεημοσυνην, a notoriously difficult phrase. Literally: "the things being within, give [as] alms." This may mean: (a) give the contents of the cup/dish as alms, (b) give from your inner disposition (i.e., give with a sincere heart), or (c) a mistranslation of an Aramaic original meaning "cleanse the inside" (dakki/zakki confusion). The saying contrasts outer ritual purification with inner moral transformation through generosity.

24. **11:42 — "rue" (πηγανον):** Sinaiticus reads πηγανον ("rue"), a strong-smelling herb used medicinally. The Matthean parallel (23:23) reads ανηθον ("dill") instead. Both lists illustrate the absurdity of tithing garden herbs while neglecting the weightier matters. The combination of mint, rue, and "every herb" (παν λαχανον) reflects Palestinian agricultural practice.

25. **11:44 — "unseen tombs" (τα μνημεια τα αδηλα):** Sinaiticus reads τα μνημεια τα αδηλα ("the unseen/unmarked graves"). Contact with a grave caused ritual impurity for seven days (Numbers 19:16). The Matthean parallel (23:27) describes "whitewashed tombs" — visible but deceptive. Luke's image is different and arguably more devastating: the Pharisees are unmarked graves that contaminate people without their knowledge. The defilement is entirely hidden.

26. **11:45 — "law-experts" (νομικων):** Sinaiticus reads νομικων, distinguishing this group from the Pharisees. While Pharisees were a religious party defined by their approach to Torah observance, the νομικοι were professional legal scholars — experts in halakhic interpretation. The law-expert recognizes that Jesus' critique of the Pharisees implicates his own profession.

27. **11:48 — "you are witnesses and you consent" (μαρτυρες εστε και συνευδοκειτε):** Sinaiticus reads συνευδοκειτε ("you consent together/approve"). Building elaborate tombs for murdered prophets, while appearing to honor them, actually completes the fathers' work — the prophets are safely dead and domesticated. The tomb-building is an unconscious testimony that the builders share their fathers' rejection of the living prophetic word.

28. **11:49 — "the Wisdom of God said" (η σοφια του θεου ειπεν):** Sinaiticus reads η σοφια του θεου ειπεν. This quotation formula is unique — no known Jewish text contains these exact words. Options: (a) Jesus quotes a lost Jewish wisdom text, (b) Jesus speaks as the personified Wisdom of God (cf. Proverbs 8; Sirach 24; Wisdom 7-9), (c) Luke interprets a saying that Matthew 23:34 attributes directly to Jesus ("Therefore I send you prophets..."). Option (b) is theologically profound: Jesus identifies himself with divine Wisdom who sends and is rejected.

29. **11:50 — "from the foundation of the world" (απο καταβολης κοσμου):** Sinaiticus reads απο καταβολης κοσμου. This phrase frames the murder of prophets as a pattern spanning all of human history — from creation's foundation to the present generation. The accumulated guilt of centuries converges upon "this generation," which represents the culmination of Israel's prophetic rejection.

30. **11:51 — "Abel...Zechariah" (Αβελ...Ζαχαριου):** Sinaiticus reads from Abel to Zechariah, spanning the entire Hebrew canon. Abel's murder is the first recorded in Scripture (Genesis 4:8). The Zechariah in view is most likely Zechariah son of Jehoiada, stoned in the temple court (2 Chronicles 24:20-22) — 2 Chronicles being the last book in the Hebrew Bible's order. Thus "from Abel to Zechariah" means "from the first to the last murder recorded in Scripture." Sinaiticus reads "having perished" (απολομενου) rather than "having been killed," and "the house" (του οικου) rather than "the temple" (του ναου), though both refer to the temple.

31. **11:52 — "key of the knowledge" (την κλειδα της γνωσεως):** Sinaiticus reads την κλειδα της γνωσεως ("the key of the knowledge"). The Matthean parallel (23:13) speaks of "shutting the kingdom of the heavens." Luke's image is distinct: the law-experts possess a key that should unlock knowledge (of God, of salvation, of Torah's true meaning) but instead they have "taken it away" (ηρατε) — neither using it themselves nor allowing others access. The key metaphor implies stewardship of sacred knowledge, a responsibility betrayed.

32. **11:53 — "to be terribly hostile" (δεινως ενεχειν):** Sinaiticus reads δεινως ενεχειν, where δεινως means "terribly/vehemently" and ενεχειν means "to hold against/be hostile toward." The compound image is of grudge-bearing escalated to active hostility. The scribes and Pharisees shift from passive offense to aggressive verbal entrapment.

33. **11:54 — "lying in ambush" (ενεδρευοντες):** Sinaiticus reads ενεδρευοντες αυτον, from ενεδρευω ("to lie in wait/ambush"). This is hunting language — the religious leaders now stalk Jesus like prey, seeking to "catch something from his mouth" (θηρευσαι τι εκ του στοματος αυτου). The verb θηρευω ("to hunt/catch") reinforces the predatory imagery. The chapter closes with the battle lines drawn: the conflict with the religious establishment that will culminate in Jerusalem.
